articles.title: Can nighttime brain bursts predict performance on intelligence tests?
articles.summary: A large-scale statistical review reveals how the electrical rhythms of the sleeping brain change as we age. The findings also outline differences in rest architecture between men and women, alongside unique links to waking cognitive abilities.
articles.aiSummary: A large-scale statistical review reveals how the electrical rhythms of the sleeping brain change as we age. The findings also outline differences in rest architecture between men and women, alongside unique links to waking cognitive abilities.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A large-scale statistical review reveals how the electrical rhythms of the sleeping brain change as we age. The findings also outline differences in rest architecture between men and women, alongside unique links to waking cognitive abilities.

articles.title: Negative life events trigger different depressive symptoms in teenage girls and boys
articles.summary: A new study tracked teenagers over a year and found that experiencing negative life events is strongly linked to depressive symptoms. For girls, stressful events were specifically tied to a sharp increase in negative self-evaluation.
articles.aiSummary: A new study tracked teenagers over a year and found that experiencing negative life events is strongly linked to depressive symptoms. For girls, stressful events were specifically tied to a sharp increase in negative self-evaluation.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study tracked teenagers over a year and found that experiencing negative life events is strongly linked to depressive symptoms. For girls, stressful events were specifically tied to a sharp increase in negative self-evaluation.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal how uneven intelligence scores relate to attention deficits in children
articles.summary: When a child’s verbal and nonverbal intelligence scores don’t match up, it can signal underlying struggles with impulse control. New brain imaging research explores how this intelligence gap relates to blood flow in the prefrontal cortex.
articles.aiSummary: When a child’s verbal and nonverbal intelligence scores don’t match up, it can signal underlying struggles with impulse control. New brain imaging research explores how this intelligence gap relates to blood flow in the prefrontal cortex.

articles.title: Teachers say they distrust AI but still accept its harsh grading mistakes, study finds
articles.summary: When grading student work, teachers are surprisingly quick to accept an AI's overly harsh mistakes. A recent study shows that simply keeping a human in the loop isn't enough to prevent automated systems from making unfair decisions.
articles.aiSummary: When grading student work, teachers are surprisingly quick to accept an AI's overly harsh mistakes. A recent study shows that simply keeping a human in the loop isn't enough to prevent automated systems from making unfair decisions.

articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: One highly desirable trait can dominate how you choose a romantic partner
articles.summary: When evaluating potential dates, people do not simply tally up positive and negative qualities. Research shows that a single highly attractive characteristic often outweighs other factors, heavily influencing our final romantic choices.
articles.aiSummary: When evaluating potential dates, people do not simply tally up positive and negative qualities. Research shows that a single highly attractive characteristic often outweighs other factors, heavily influencing our final romantic choices.

articles.title: The science of “mommy brain”: How dopamine locks in lifelong cognitive benefits for mothers
articles.summary: A recent study published in Nature reveals that motherhood permanently remodels the brain through dopamine, enhancing learning and memory. The findings challenge the "mommy brain" myth, while warning that severe postpartum stress can disrupt these natural adaptations.
articles.aiSummary: A recent study published in Nature reveals that motherhood permanently remodels the brain through dopamine, enhancing learning and memory. The findings challenge the "mommy brain" myth, while warning that severe postpartum stress can disrupt these natural adaptations.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A recent study published in Nature reveals that motherhood permanently remodels the brain through dopamine, enhancing learning and memory. The findings challenge the "mommy brain" myth, while warning that severe postpartum stress can disrupt these natural adaptations.

articles.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
articles.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

articles.title: Study links autistic traits to how the brain processes social versus nonsocial rewards
articles.summary: New brain imaging research reveals that individuals with higher autistic traits show weaker connections between visual and reward-processing brain regions, explaining why they may place less value on social rewards like smiles compared to nonsocial rewards.
articles.aiSummary: New brain imaging research reveals that individuals with higher autistic traits show weaker connections between visual and reward-processing brain regions, explaining why they may place less value on social rewards like smiles compared to nonsocial rewards.

articles.title: People with insecure relationship habits tend to have more children, study finds
articles.summary: Research across Japan, Canada, and the United States shows that fearful and preoccupied relationship traits actually predict larger family sizes. The findings suggest cultural norms heavily influence how our emotional habits impact reproductive success.
articles.aiSummary: Research across Japan, Canada, and the United States shows that fearful and preoccupied relationship traits actually predict larger family sizes. The findings suggest cultural norms heavily influence how our emotional habits impact reproductive success.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Research across Japan, Canada, and the United States shows that fearful and preoccupied relationship traits actually predict larger family sizes. The findings suggest cultural norms heavily influence how our emotional habits impact reproductive success.

articles.title: An international brain imaging analysis reveals how psychedelics rewire neural circuits
articles.summary: A new analysis of combined brain imaging data from 273 people reveals how classic psychedelics flatten the brain’s strict processing hierarchy, increasing communication between sensory systems and the cognitive regions responsible for abstract thought.
articles.aiSummary: A new analysis of combined brain imaging data from 273 people reveals how classic psychedelics flatten the brain’s strict processing hierarchy, increasing communication between sensory systems and the cognitive regions responsible for abstract thought.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new analysis of combined brain imaging data from 273 people reveals how classic psychedelics flatten the brain’s strict processing hierarchy, increasing communication between sensory systems and the cognitive regions responsible for abstract thought.

articles.title: People prefer negotiating with women over men, study suggests
articles.summary: New research shows that people walk away from deals more satisfied when their counterpart is a woman. Women achieve the exact same financial results as men but excel at building relationships, leading to more future business opportunities.
articles.aiSummary: New research shows that people walk away from deals more satisfied when their counterpart is a woman. Women achieve the exact same financial results as men but excel at building relationships, leading to more future business opportunities.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. articles.title: Eight weeks of guided slow breathing alters stress responses in veterans
articles.summary: A daily routine of device-guided slow breathing helps veterans with trauma disorders dial back their physical fight-or-flight response to sudden mental stress, suggesting a new path for protecting the cardiovascular health of vulnerable patients without relying on medications.
articles.aiSummary: A daily routine of device-guided slow breathing helps veterans with trauma disorders dial back their physical fight-or-flight response to sudden mental stress, suggesting a new path for protecting the cardiovascular health of vulnerable patients without relying on medications.

articles.title: How people interpret life milestones is tied to how their personalities develop
articles.summary: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.
articles.aiSummary: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.

articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

articles.title: Baby teeth reveal how early metal exposures shape the adolescent brain
articles.summary: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.
articles.aiSummary: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.

articles.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.
